name: Mining & Extractives official sources
notes: Mining royalty rates are legally significant and perishable: they are set by each jurisdiction's Mining Code / Finance Act and revised at budget. Several regimes (South Africa, Zambia copper, Burkina Faso gold, Zimbabwe gold) use price- or profit-based sliding formulas, not a flat rate.

data/mining/mining-royalties.js carries one lastUpdated stamp for all rows — a floor, not a per-figure date. Rates are presented in the Mining Royalty Calculator as EDITABLE, planning-grade defaults the user must confirm, never as current gazetted rates.

Never change a royalty rate because a URL's content hash changed. Open the Mining Code / Finance Act / regulator schedule and read it, exactly as .claude/rules/government.md and .claude/rules/energy.md require.
A ministry, revenue authority or minerals-commission source outranks any aggregator, law-firm summary or news report for the same claim. Law-firm mining guides are a sanity check, never a citation.

The bug class to watch: `country.minerals[m].rate || 0` turns a missing or formula-based rate into 0%, which makes the royalty vanish and understates cost. The calculator instead FORCES the user to enter a rate when none is bound, and shows the min/max band for sliding-scale regimes — copy that pattern, never default a rate to zero.

Only 13 of Africa's ~35 significant mining jurisdictions have a bound royalty source today. The rest are recorded in gaps.jurisdictionsWithoutSource as known liabilities, not to-dos. Do not present an unbound market's rate as fact.

sources.authority: Ghana Minerals Commission / Ghana Revenue Authority
sources.title: Minerals and Mining Act 2006 (Act 703) — mineral royalty
sources.notes: Ghana applies a flat 5% ad-valorem royalty on the gross value of all minerals under Act 703, s.25. Confirm against the current Act before changing the rate.

sources.authority: Mining Commission of Tanzania (Tume ya Madini)
sources.title: Mining Act (as amended 2017) — royalty schedule
sources.notes: 6% on metallic minerals and gemstones/diamonds under the 2017 amendments, plus a separate 1% clearing-house inspection fee on gross value. Confirm the current schedule.

sources.title: Mineral & Petroleum Resources Royalty Act 28 of 2008
sources.notes: Profit-based formula, NOT a flat rate: refined minerals 0.5%–5%, unrefined 0.5%–7% of gross sales. The calculator shows the band and requires the user to enter their effective rate.

sources.authority: Zambia Ministry of Mines and Minerals Development / ZRA
sources.title: Mines and Minerals Development Act — royalty
sources.notes: Copper royalty is a price-linked sliding scale 4%–10% (LME band); base metals 5%, precious metals and gemstones 6%. Confirm the current copper price band before quoting a copper rate.

sources.authority: DRC Ministère des Mines / Cadastre Minier (CAMI)
sources.title: Code Minier 2018 (Loi n°18/001)
sources.notes: 2018 Mining Code: precious metals 3.5%, base metals 3.5%, strategic substances (cobalt, coltan) 10%, precious stones 6%, ferrous 1%. Confirm the strategic-substance list, which the government can revise by decree.

sources.authority: Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (ZIMRA) / Ministry of Mines
sources.title: Finance Act — mining royalties
sources.notes: Rates are set in the Finance Act and revised at each national budget — treat as high-perishability. Gold has a reduced small-scale rate. Confirm the current budget.

sources.authority: Botswana Ministry of Minerals and Energy
sources.title: Mines and Minerals Act — royalty
sources.notes: Precious stones 10%, precious metals 5%, other minerals 3% of gross market value. Confirm against the current Act.

sources.authority: Namibia Ministry of Mines and Energy
sources.title: Minerals (Prospecting and Mining) Act — royalty
sources.notes: Rough diamonds 10%, precious/base metals 3%, semi-precious stones 2%. Namibia has periodically proposed changes — confirm current rates.

sources.authority: Nigeria Mining Cadastre Office / Ministry of Solid Minerals Development
sources.title: Nigerian Minerals and Mining Regulations 2011 — royalty schedule
sources.notes: Royalties are set per mineral in the 2011 Regulations schedule. Confirm the per-mineral rate for solid minerals before quoting.

sources.title: Mining Act 2016 — royalty schedule
sources.notes: Mining Act 2016 schedule: gold 5%, titanium ores 10%, coal 8%, gemstones 5%. Confirm the current schedule.

sources.title: Code Minier du Mali — redevances
sources.notes: Gold ad-valorem royalty ~3% plus additional levies (ISCP/CPS) under the Mining Code. Mali revised its Mining Code in 2023 — confirm the current version and the additional levies.

sources.authority: Burkina Faso Ministère de l'Énergie, des Mines et des Carrières
sources.title: Code Minier du Burkina Faso — redevances proportionnelles
sources.notes: Gold royalty is a price-linked sliding scale 3%–5%. Confirm the current price thresholds in the Mining Code.

jurisdictionsWithoutSource.authority: Guinea Ministère des Mines et de la Géologie
Angola Ministério dos Recursos Minerais, Petróleo e Gás
Mozambique Ministério dos Recursos Minerais e Energia
Côte d'Ivoire Ministère des Mines, du Pétrole et de l'Énergie
Mauritania Ministère du Pétrole, des Mines et de l'Énergie
Liberia Ministry of Mines and Energy

unsourcedClaims.why: South Africa (profit formula), Zambia copper, Burkina Faso gold and Zimbabwe gold use price- or profit-based formulas. The calculator shows the statutory band and requires the user to confirm their effective rate rather than asserting a single flat number.

Surface rent, education/inspection levies and additional taxes (e.g. Mali ISCP/CPS, Tanzania 1% clearing fee) vary by jurisdiction and are only partially modelled. Only Tanzania's 1% clearing fee is bound today; the rest are out of scope for the royalty figure.
